moveAnnotations.annotation: We're playing the Zukertort Opening, named after the legendary 19th-century master Wilhelm Zukertort—a flexible system that delays committing the center pawns and keeps maximum options open. By developing Nf3 immediately, we control the key central squares e5 and d4 while preparing to support our center with moves like c4 or d4 later. This move is incredibly practical because it doesn't telegraph our plan, and the knight is well-placed on f3 regardless of which setup we eventually choose.

Black responds with the Dutch Variation, immediately staking a claim in the center with a pawn and preparing ...e6 or ...e5 to establish a strong kingside presence. This move is thematic for Dutch Defense players—it gains space on the f-file and the long diagonal while setting up quick piece development. However, it does create a slight weakness on e6 and along the long diagonal, which you should keep in mind as the position develops; you may want to exploit this with moves like c4 or d4 combined with developing your dark-squared bishop.
